html:
Anna Gustavsdotter Vasa (also known as Anna Maria), born June 19, 1545, and died March 20, 1610, was a Swedish princess and Countess Palatine of the County of Veldenz. She was the daughter of Gustav Vasa and Margareta Eriksdotter (Leijonhufvud), and married on December 20, 1562, to Count Palatine Georg Johan I of Pfalz-Veldenz. She served as interim regent in Pfalz-Veldenz from 1592 to 1598, while awaiting the division of her husband's territory among his sons.

== Biography ==

=== Background === Very little is known about Anna's childhood. In 1547, she was ill at Svartsjö Castle. Like her sisters, she likely received instruction in the German language. After her mother's death in 1551, she was cared for by Kristina Gyllenstierna and then by her aunts, Queen Märta and Brita Leijonhufvud, before her father remarried to Katarina Stenbock.

From an early age, her father began planning her marriage. In 1556, portraits of her and her sisters were painted and sent throughout Europe to promote them on the political marriage market, with promises of a dowry of 100,000 daler and poems by the court poet Henricus Mollerus that highlighted their virtues. The same year, a portrait of both Anna and her sister Katarina was sent to East Frisia. However, it was Katarina who was chosen for that marriage. In 1559, Gustav Vasa suggested Ludwig VI of Palatinate as a potential husband for either Anna or her sister Cecilia. If Cecilia were chosen, Count Poppo XII of Hennberg, who had become a widower in 1558, was proposed instead of Anna. Georg Johan I of Pfalz-Veldenz, who had previously planned a marriage with Cecilia Vasa, withdrew after the events at Vadstena and then chose Anna instead. The marriage contract was signed on October 3, 1562, and the wedding ceremony took place at Stockholm Castle on December 20 of the same year. The purpose of the marriage was to secure contacts for Sweden among the German dynasties, as Georg Johan was related to most of the most influential families in Germany.

=== Countess of Palatinate === Anna received a dowry of 100,000 guilders, which was significantly higher than the typical dowry for a German princess, who usually received no more than 28,000. Her wedding was celebrated with greater pomp than that of her sister Cecilia: the Danish monarch, among others, attended the ceremony, and Anna wore a pearl crown. After the wedding, the couple remained in Sweden for some time so that Anna's dowry could be assessed, and they did not leave Sweden until July 1563. After a tour of Germany, they settled in Lauterecken in Palatinate-Veldenz.

The marriage is considered to have been happy. Anna reportedly acted as her husband's advisor and tried to temper his adventurous projects. In connection with her brother Karl's trip to Germany in 1577, Johann Georg Johan was suspected of participating in a conspiracy with Karl against him, and Anna had to act as a mediator. During 1578–1579, she is said to have conducted the negotiations for the marriage between her brother Karl IX and Maria of Palatinate. At the same time, Anna's dowry appears to have been depleted, as Johann Georg Johan could not afford to attend Karl's wedding in 1579.

Anna's unusually large dowry was used by her husband for a very lavish court life and ruinous speculations. Her husband incurred significant debts due to his extravagant lifestyle and reckless policies, which severely damaged the state's economy. This included loans taken out to finance projects or pay off existing debts. Johann Georg Johan's perhaps greatest project was the city of Pfalzburg, which he founded in northern Alsace and inaugurated in 1570, where the couple occasionally resided at the castle of La Petite Pierre (Lützelstien) just outside the city. Anna was often tasked with acting as a mediator on her husband's behalf, particularly when it came to his business ventures. In 1588, she was asked to persuade the Duke of Lorraine to extend the deadline for the repurchase of Pfalzburg, which Johann Georg Johan had sold to Lorraine with the right of redemption, so that he would have time to raise the money needed for a repurchase. She succeeded in extending the deadline, but Pfalzburg ultimately passed to Lorraine in 1590.

=== Regency === Upon her husband's death in April 1592, the state was ruined: the interest payments on the debts alone exceeded the state's income. In order to save money, she lived with her in-laws for several years before re-establishing the court. She initiated negotiations with both Gunilla Bielke and Karl IX to obtain the funds that Johan III had promised her from Sweden.

There were also disputes over the inheritance between the sons, which Anna tried to resolve. Georg Johan had divided his possessions among the sons, which led to the inheritance dispute. The eldest son, Georg Gustav, was to receive Veldenz, while Johan August Lützelstein received other holdings, and Anna served as the administrative regent for the entire territory from 1592 until it could finally be divided among the sons in 1598. She unsuccessfully tried to persuade her brother Karl to participate in the regency. In collaboration with the other regents, her brothers-in-law Richard of Simmern and Ludwig of Württemberg, and Ernst Philip of Baden, she managed to resolve the problems. As regent, she threatened the Duke of Lorraine in 1592 with revenge from her ruling position.

How certain are we about this?

From the Vasa era onwards, individuals are well-documented in archives. The uncertainty lies more in interpretation and the post-mortem perception – not least because those in power themselves were involved in shaping their own historical narratives.
tldr: Anna Gustavsdotter Vasa was a Swedish princess who married a German prince to establish important connections in Europe. After his death, she became the leader of his territory and worked diligently to resolve economic problems and inheritance disputes.
lattlast:
Anna was the daughter of King Gustav Vasa. Through her marriage to the German prince George John, she received a very large sum of money as a dowry. The purpose of the marriage was for Sweden to establish better connections with powerful families in Germany.

After the marriage, Anna moved to Germany. Her husband used her money to build cities and live a luxurious life, which led to significant debts. Anna often had to help him negotiate with other leaders when he faced problems with his business or finances.

When her husband died in 1592, Anna became responsible for governing the region temporarily. This was a difficult situation because the state lacked funds, and there were disputes between her sons about who would inherit what. She worked with other officials to resolve these conflicts and arrange the inheritance so that it could eventually be divided among the sons.
